info.set_name: Gastrointestinal Comprehensive Set 1: Secretory Mechanisms (Short Answer)

q1.prompt: Starting with the salivary glands, describe the two-step process of saliva formation, specifically identifying the roles of acinar cells versus ductal cells.
correctOptionIds: Acinar cells produce an isotonic, plasma-like primary secretion. Ductal cells reabsorb Na^+ and Cl^- and secrete K^+ and HCO_3^-.

q2.prompt: Under basal conditions, how does the ionic composition (Na^+, Cl^-, K^+, HCO_3^-) and tonicity of saliva compare to that of plasma?
correctOptionIds: Saliva is hypotonic; it has higher [K^+] and [HCO_3^-] and lower [Na^+] and [Cl^-] than plasma.

q3.prompt: Explain why the bicarbonate concentration in saliva increases as the flow rate increases, despite the "contact time" theory.
correctOptionIds: HCO_3^- is the only ion whose secretion is specifically stimulated by agonists (like parasympathetic activity) as the flow rate increases.

q4.prompt: Identify the unique characteristic of salivary gland regulation regarding the roles of the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems.
correctOptionIds: Saliva production is increased by both the sympathetic and parasympathetic systems (parasympathetic is dominant).

q5.prompt: Which enzyme is responsible for starting carbohydrate digestion in the mouth, and at what pH does it become inactivated?
correctOptionIds: α-amylase (ptyalin); it is inactivated when the pH falls below 4.0.

q6.prompt: List the four major components of gastric juice and their respective cells of origin.
correctOptionIds: HCl and Intrinsic Factor (Parietal cells), Pepsinogen (Chief cells), and Mucus (Mucous neck cells).

q7.prompt: Describe the cellular mechanism of the "alkaline tide" observed in gastric venous blood following a meal.
correctOptionIds: For every H^+ secreted into the lumen, one HCO_3^- is absorbed into the blood via the Cl^-/HCO_3^- exchanger on the basolateral membrane.

q8.prompt: Explain the function of the H^+/K^+ ATPase (proton pump) in the apical membrane of parietal cells.
correctOptionIds: It is a primary active transport process that pumps H^+ into the lumen and K^+ into the cell against their gradients.

q9.prompt: Why is intrinsic factor considered the only "essential" component of gastric juice?
correctOptionIds: It is required for the absorption of vitamin B12 in the ileum; without it, pernicious anemia develops.

q10.prompt: Name the three primary physiological agonists that stimulate H^+ secretion by parietal cells.
correctOptionIds: Histamine, Acetylcholine (ACh), and Gastrin.

q11.prompt: Define "potentiation" in the context of gastric acid secretion and explain why H_2-receptor antagonists are so effective.
correctOptionIds: The response to multiple stimulants is greater than the sum of their individual effects. H_2 blockers are effective because they block the direct and potentiated effects of histamine.

q12.prompt: Contrast the "direct" and "indirect" pathways by which the vagus nerve stimulates gastric acid secretion.
correctOptionIds: Direct: Vagus releases ACh on parietal cells. Indirect: Vagus releases GRP on G cells to stimulate gastrin release.

q13.prompt: Describe the negative feedback mechanism involving the antral pH and somatostatin.
correctOptionIds: Low pH stimulates D cells to release somatostatin, which directly inhibits parietal cells and indirectly inhibits them by suppressing gastrin release.

q14.prompt: Identify the three phases of gastric acid secretion.
correctOptionIds: Cephalic (30%), Gastric (60%), and Intestinal (10%).

q15.prompt: What is the inactive precursor of pepsin, and how is it activated in the gastric lumen?
correctOptionIds: Pepsinogen; it is activated by low gastric pH (<5) and by existing pepsin.

q16.prompt: Compare the primary functions of pancreatic acinar cells versus pancreatic ductal cells.
correctOptionIds: Acinar cells secrete enzymes (lipases, amylases, proteases). Ductal cells secrete the aqueous HCO_3^- component.

q17.prompt: Explain how the ionic composition of pancreatic juice (specifically HCO^3_- and Cl^-) changes as the flow rate increases.
correctOptionIds: As flow rate increases, [HCO_3^-] increases and [Cl^-] decreases; Na^+ and K^+ remain constant.

q18.prompt: Name the major hormone that stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion and the specific luminal stimulus for its release.
correctOptionIds: Secretin; it is released in response to H^+ (acid) in the duodenal lumen.

q19.prompt: Which duodenal hormone is the primary stimulus for the secretion of pancreatic enzymes during the intestinal phase?
correctOptionIds: Cholecystokinin (CCK).

q20.prompt: Explain the role of enterokinase (enteropeptidase) in the activation of pancreatic proteases.
correctOptionIds: Enterokinase activates trypsinogen to trypsin, which then activates all other proenzymes.

q21.prompt: What are the primary organic constituents of bile, and which is the most abundant?
correctOptionIds: Bile salts (50%), phospholipids (40%), cholesterol (4%), and bilirubin (2%).

q22.prompt: Contrast primary bile acids with secondary bile acids, including the role of intestinal bacteria in their formation.
correctOptionIds: Primary acids (cholic, chenodeoxycholic) are made by the liver. Secondary acids (deoxycholic, lithocholic) are produced by bacterial dehydroxylation in the gut.

q23.prompt: Why are bile salts more effective for lipid digestion in the duodenal environment than bile acids.
correctOptionIds: Salts have lower pKs (1-4) and remain ionized/soluble at duodenal pH, whereas acids (pK ~7) would be non-ionized/insoluble.

q24.prompt: Describe the two coordinated actions of cholecystokinin (CCK) that result in the ejection of bile into the duodenum.
correctOptionIds: CCK causes contraction of the gallbladder and relaxation of the sphincter of Oddi.

q25.prompt: Explain the "enterohepatic circulation" of bile salts and identify the primary site of their reabsorption.
correctOptionIds: It is the recycling of bile salts from the terminal ileum back to the liver via the portal circulation.

q26.prompt: How does the liver handle the metabolic byproduct of hemoglobin (bilirubin) before secreting it into the bile?
correctOptionIds: Hemoglobin is degraded to biliverdin, then bilirubin; the liver conjugates it with glucuronic acid to make it water-soluble.

q27.prompt: Identify the enzyme that serves as the rate-limiting step for bile acid synthesis and explain how it is regulated.
correctOptionIds: Cholesterol 7α-hydroxylase; it is inhibited by high levels of recirculated bile salts (negative feedback).

q28.prompt: What is the function of "monitor peptide" and "CCK-releasing peptide" in the regulation of pancreatic secretion.
correctOptionIds: They are small peptides that stimulate CCK release and ensure enzyme secretion matches protein intake; they are eventually digested by proteases.

q29.prompt: Describe the role of colipase in fat digestion.
correctOptionIds: Colipase displaces bile salts from fat droplets so that pancreatic lipase can bind and digest the triglycerides.

q30.prompt: List the major factors that can weaken the gastric mucosal barrier, predisposing a patient to ulceration.
correctOptionIds: H. pylori, aspirin/NSAIDs, ethanol, bile salts, and stress.
